request.question: Were both Elizabeth Chadwick and Connie May Fowler authors?

title: Nancy Fairbanks
Elizabeth Chadwick
Beale family
Alan Chadwick
Connie May Fowler
Stephen F. Chadwick
Theophilus Hastings, 7th Earl of Huntingdon
Before Women Had Wings
Fowler's position
Owen Chadwick
sentences: Nancy Fairbanks Herndon is a US lecturer in English and the author of historical romances under the pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick and mysteries under the name Nancy Herndon and Nancy Fairbanks.
Elizabeth Chadwick is an author of historical fictions.
She is a member of Regia Anglorum, a Medieval reenactment organisation.
The Beales, together with the Fowlers, are a fictional family in the BBC soap opera "EastEnders".
They were the main family for storylines in early "EastEnders" and remained as such for many years.
Having appeared continuously from episode one, they are the show's longest serving family.
In 2004 Peter Beale and Lucy Beale were recast to give them a more mature look.
In 2007, there were few members of the Beale family left and no Fowlers, so the family were revamped.
Peter was recast again.
Ian Beale started a relationship with Jane Beale and Steven Beale also returned.
By 2010, the number of Beales had fallen again.
Lucy was recast and in 2013, Peter and Bobby were recast and Cindy Williams Jnr, the half-sister of Peter and Lucy, was reintroduced.
In addition to this at the start of 2014 Bex Fowler returned to the serial with her mother Sonia Fowler, with Martin Fowler following 11 months later in December.
In 2014 Lucy is found dead, causing her family and friends to struggle to come to terms with her death and the secrets she left behind, putting many people in the frame for her murder.
It was revealed on 19 February 2015 that she was killed by her own little brother, Bobby.
It was also revealed that Kathy Beale, who was presumed dead in 2006, was alive after having faked her death with her new husband in South Africa.
In December 2016 Michelle Fowler returned to Albert Square after originally leaving in 1995, although now played by Jenna Russell.
Members of the Beale family by blood currently in "EastEnders" include Ian, Michelle, Martin, Bex and Louie.
Additionally, several others may be considered Beales through marriage; Jane, Kathy, Sonia and Stacey Fowler are among these.
Also, Whitney Dean was adopted by Bianca Butcher, who is a member of the Beale family.
Lily Fowler and Arthur Fowler have both taken Martin's surname and have him as their stepfather.

Alan Chadwick (July 27, 1909 – May 25, 1980), English master gardener, was a leading innovator of organic farming techniques and influential educator in the field of biodynamic/French intensive gardening.
He was a student of Rudolf Steiner and is often cited as inspirational to the development of the "California Cuisine" movement.
The Chadwick restaurant in Beverly Hills was named after him.
His grave is marked by a stupa at the Green Gulch Farm Zen Center in California.

Chadwick is the subject of a 2013 retrospective by a former University of California, Santa Cruz professor, Paul Lee, called "There Is a Garden in the Mind: A Memoir of Alan Chadwick and the Organic Movement in California".
There is a memorial page at http://www.alan-chadwick.org/ that includes the 1971 16-mm film "Garden".
Connie May Fowler (born January 3, 1960 to parents of multi-cultural backgrounds) is an American novelist, essayist, memoirist, screenwriter, and poet.
Her semi-autobiographical novel, "Before Women had Wings", received the 1996 Southern Book Critics Circle Award and the Francis Buck Award (League of American Pen Women).
She adapted the novel for Oprah Winfrey and the subsequent Emmy-winning film starred Winfrey, Ellen Barkin, Julia Stiles, and Tina Majorino.
"Remembering Blue" received the Chautauqua South Literary Award.
Three of her novels were Dublin International Literary Award nominees.
Her other novels include "Sugar Cage" and "River of Hidden Dreams".
"The Problem with Murmur Lee" was Redbook’s premier book club selection.
Her memoir, "When Katie Wakes", explores her family’s generational cycle of domestic violence.
"How Clarissa Burden Learned to Fly", a novel oft compared to Virginia Woolf's "Mrs. Dalloway" in term of its structure, was published in 2010.
Her latest book, a memoir titled "A Million Fragile bones," will be published April 20, 2017 by Twisted Road Publications.
It explores her life on an isolated barrier island and the horrific impact and aftermath of the Deepwater Horizon BP oil spill.
Her books have been translated into eighteen languages (http://www.conniemayfowler.com/about.html).
Stephen Fowler Chadwick (December 25, 1825 – January 15, 1895) was an American Democratic politician who served as the fifth Governor of Oregon from 1877 to 1878.
Governor Chadwick was the first person to obtain the governorship by way of the state's Line of Succession.
Theophilus Hastings, 7th Earl of Huntingdon PC (10 December 1650 – 30 May 1701) was an English politician.
He was the son of Ferdinando Hastings, 6th Earl of Huntingdon, born in the 27th year of his parents' marriage, and became Earl of Huntingdon on 13 February 1656 on his father's death.
He married, firstly, Elizabeth Lewis, daughter of Sir John Lewis, 1st Baronet, on 19 February 1671/2.
She died 24th December 1688.
He married, secondly, Mary Frances Fowler, daughter of Francis Leveson Fowler, on 8 May 1690.
With his first wife he had two sons and six daughters, including Thomas (1674–1675), George and Elizabeth.
With his second wife he had two more sons and five more daughters, including Theophilus, Catherine, Maria and Margaret.
Before Women Had Wings is a 1997 television film, based on the story by Connie May Fowler about a mother whose abusive husband commits suicide.
She then starts to violently abuse her two daughters.
It is only then that a kindly black woman becomes good friends with her youngest daughter and helps the daughter escape her tortured life.
The film stars Ellen Barkin, Oprah Winfrey, Tina Majorino, and Julia Stiles.
Barkin won an Emmy for her role.
In medicine, Fowler's position is a standard patient position.
It is an intervention used to promote oxygenation via maximum chest expansion and is implemented during events of respiratory distress.
Fowler's position facilitates the relaxing of tension of the abdominal muscles, allowing for improved breathing.
In immobile patients and infants, the Fowler's position alleviates compression of the chest that occurs due to gravity.
Fowler's position increases comfort during eating and other activities, is used in postpartum women to improve uterine drainage, and in infants when signs of respiratory distress present.
The patient is placed in a semi-upright sitting position (45-60 degrees) and may have knees either bent or straight.
Fowler's position is also used when oral or nasal gastric feeding tubes have been implemented as it minimizes the risk of aspiration.
Peristalsis and swallowing are aided by the effect of gravitational pull.
William Owen Chadwick ( ; 20 May 1916 – 17 July 2015) was a British Anglican priest, academic, writer and prominent historian of Christianity.
As a leading academic, Chadwick became Dixie Professor of Ecclesiastical History from 1958 to 1968 and Regius Professor of History from 1968 to 1983.
From 1956 to 1983, Chadwick was elected and severed as the Master of Selwyn College, Cambridge.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

hop1_docs: Elizabeth Chadwick | Elizabeth Chadwick is an author of historical fictions.  She is a member of Regia Anglorum, a Medieval reenactment organisation.

Stephen F. Chadwick | Stephen Fowler Chadwick (December 25, 1825 – January 15, 1895) was an American Democratic politician who served as the fifth Governor of Oregon from 1877 to 1878.  Governor Chadwick was the first person to obtain the governorship by way of the state's Line of Succession.
Lilian Fowler | Elizabeth Lilian Maud Fowler MBE, JP (7 June 1886 – 11 May 1954) was an Australian politician, and Australia's first female mayor.

Nancy Fairbanks | Nancy Fairbanks Herndon is a US lecturer in English and the author of historical romances under the pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick and mysteries under the name Nancy Herndon and Nancy Fairbanks.

Connie May Fowler | Connie May Fowler (born January 3, 1960 to parents of multi-cultural backgrounds) is an American novelist, essayist, memoirist, screenwriter, and poet.  Her semi-autobiographical novel, "Before Women had Wings", received the 1996 Southern Book Critics Circle Award and the Francis Buck Award (League of American Pen Women).  She adapted the novel for Oprah Winfrey and the subsequent Emmy-winning film starred Winfrey, Ellen Barkin, Julia Stiles, and Tina Majorino.  "Remembering Blue" received the Chautauqua South Literary Award.  Three of her novels were Dublin International Literary Award nominees.  Her other novels include "Sugar Cage" and "River of Hidden Dreams".  "The Problem with Murmur Lee" was Redbook’s premier book club selection.  Her memoir, "When Katie Wakes", explores her family’s generational cycle of domestic violence.  "How Clarissa Burden Learned to Fly", a novel oft compared to Virginia Woolf's "Mrs. Dalloway" in term of its structure, was published in 2010.  Her latest book, a memoir titled "A Million Fragile bones," will be published April 20, 2017 by Twisted Road Publications.  It explores her life on an isolated barrier island and the horrific impact and aftermath of the Deepwater Horizon BP oil spill.  Her books have been translated into eighteen languages (http://www.conniemayfowler.com/about.html).

Connie Needham | Connie Needham (born December 5, 1959) is an American actress and dance instructor.  As Connie Newton, she portrayed Elizabeth Bradford on the television series "Eight is Enough" from 1977 to 1981.
Conny Van Dyke | Conny Van Dyke (sometimes credited as Connie Van Dyke) is a singer and actress, born September 28, 1945 in Nassawadox, Virginia to Benjamin and Charlotte Elizabeth Van Dyke.
response.summary_1: Evidence that both are authors: [1] "Elizabeth Chadwick is an author of historical fictions." [5] "Connie May Fowler ... is an American novelist, essayist, memoirist, screenwriter, and poet." No further retrieval or disambiguation required to confirm both are authors.

Her semi-autobiographical novel, "Before Women had Wings", received the 1996 Southern Book Critics Circle Award and the Francis Buck Award (League of American Pen Women).  She adapted the novel for Oprah Winfrey and the subsequent Emmy-winning film starred Winfrey, Ellen Barkin, Julia Stiles, and Tina Majorino. Her books have been translated into eighteen languages (http://www.conniemayfowler.com/about.html).
Frances Mayes | Frances Mayes is an American university professor, poet, memoirist, essayist, and novelist.
Elizabeth L. Silver | Elizabeth L. Silver (born 1978) is an American novelist, essayist, memoirist and attorney.
Elizabeth Chadwick | Elizabeth Chadwick is an author of historical fictions.  She is a member of Regia Anglorum, a Medieval reenactment organisation.
Kate Jennings | Kate Jennings (born 20 May 1948) is an Australian poet, essayist, memoirist, and novelist.

Elizabeth Gilbert | Elizabeth M. Gilbert (born July 18, 1969) is an American author, essayist, short story writer, biographer, novelist, and memoirist.  She is best known for her 2006 memoir, "Eat, Pray, Love", which as of December 2010 had spent 199 weeks on the "New York Times Best Seller list", and which was also made into a film by the same name in 2010.

William Keepers Maxwell Jr. | William Keepers Maxwell, Jr. (August 16, 1908 – July 31, 2000) was an American editor, novelist, short story writer, essayist, children's author, and memoirist.  He served as a fiction editor at "The New Yorker" from 1936 to 1975.  An editor devoted to his writers, Maxwell became a legendary mentor and confidant to many of the most prominent authors of his day.  Although best known as an editor, Maxwell was a highly respected and award winning novelist and short story writer.  His stature as a celebrated author has grown in the years following his death.
response.summary_2: Yes — both are authors: Elizabeth Chadwick is an author of historical fiction (passage [4]) and Connie May Fowler is an American novelist, essayist, memoirist, screenwriter, and poet (passage [1]).
